hypotheses.description: DNS cache bypass / amplification attack
Misconfigured internal resolver creating query storms
Legitimate organic traffic growth

steps.rationale: Sample recent DNS queries to look for amplification patterns
steps.compressed_evidence: 2000 query sample. Top 2 domains account for 87% of volume. 94% of queries originate from public resolvers (8.8.8.8: 42%, 8.8.4.4: 31%, 1.1.1.1: 21%). Random case variations detected (e.g. ExAmPlE.cOm) — classic cache-bypass technique. Internal resolver traffic only 6%.

hypotheses.description: DNS cache bypass / amplification attack
Misconfigured internal resolver creating query storms
Legitimate organic traffic growth

steps.command: bq query --nouse_legacy_sql 'SELECT DATE(timestamp) AS day, COUNT(*) AS queries FROM dns_logs.queries WHERE timestamp > TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 14 DAY) GROUP BY day ORDER BY day'
steps.rationale: Confirm the spike start date and volume curve
steps.compressed_evidence: Daily query counts: 03-20 to 03-26 averaged 2.0M/day. 03-27: 41M (20x). 03-28: 102M. 03-29: 218M. 03-30: 351M. 03-31: 436M. Spike began precisely on 2026-03-27, matching the cost timeline.

hypotheses.description: DNS cache bypass / amplification attack
Misconfigured internal resolver creating query storms

evidence_summary: Query volume rose from 2M/day baseline to 436M/day (218x) starting 2026-03-27
94% of queries from public resolvers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4, 1.1.1.1) — only 6% internal
Random case variations in domain names — textbook cache-bypass technique
Top 2 domains account for 87% of total volume — narrow attack target
not_verified: Direct flooding vs reflection/amplification via spoofed sources
recommendations: Enable Cloud Armor rate limiting on the affected DNS zones immediately
Front DNS with Cloudflare or another DDoS-mitigation provider
Add an alerting policy for DNS query volume > 5x baseline

metadata.description: Cloud NAT egress runaway from Cloud Functions

hypotheses.description: A Cloud Function is fetching large objects from public APIs in a tight loop
GKE pods pulling images through NAT instead of Artifact Registry
Legitimate growth in outbound API integrations

steps.rationale: Identify which VMs/instances are consuming the most NAT mappings
steps.compressed_evidence: Top NAT consumer: serverless VPC connector 'svc-conn-prod' with 18,400 active mappings (94% of total). Second highest: gke-prod-pool-1 at 380 mappings (1.9%). Serverless connector traffic has grown from 200 mappings 14 days ago to 18,400 today.

hypotheses.description: A Cloud Function is fetching large objects from public APIs in a tight loop
GKE pods pulling images through NAT instead of Artifact Registry
Legitimate growth in outbound API integrations

steps.rationale: Find which Cloud Functions are routed through that VPC connector
steps.compressed_evidence: 3 functions on svc-conn-prod: 'image-thumbnailer' (updated 2026-03-26, 2 days before spike), 'webhook-fanout', 'pdf-renderer'. The image-thumbnailer was last modified the day before the cost increase began.

hypotheses.description: image-thumbnailer Cloud Function is downloading images through NAT in a tight loop after a 2026-03-26 deploy

conclude.root_cause: image-thumbnailer Cloud Function deployed 2026-03-26 is fetching source images from a public CDN through Cloud NAT, with no caching and no concurrency cap
evidence_summary: 94% of NAT mappings come from a single serverless VPC connector
Traffic on that connector grew 92x in 14 days
image-thumbnailer was deployed the day before the spike began
Other functions on the same connector show normal activity
not_verified: Whether the source images could be served from a private GCS bucket instead
Whether retries are amplifying the volume
recommendations: Roll back image-thumbnailer to the pre-2026-03-26 version while investigating
Cache fetched images in GCS and reference them via private networking
Add a per-function NAT mapping alert at 1000 active mappings

metadata.description: BigQuery on-demand spend from a recurring full table scan

hypotheses.description: A scheduled query is doing a full table scan against a partitioned table
A new BI dashboard is querying without partition filters
Storage growth pushed an existing query above the price tier

steps.command: bq query --nouse_legacy_sql 'SELECT user_email, job_id, total_bytes_billed/POW(1024,4) AS tib_billed, query FROM `region-us.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT` WHERE creation_time > TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) ORDER BY total_bytes_billed DESC LIMIT 20'
steps.rationale: Find the highest-spend queries in the last 7 days
steps.compressed_evidence: Top query by far: 47.2 TiB billed per run, run by service account 'looker-bi@…iam'. Same query ran 96 times in 7 days (every ~1.75h). Second-highest query: 0.4 TiB. The top query alone billed 4,531 TiB total this week, dwarfing everything else.

hypotheses.description: A scheduled query is doing a full table scan against a partitioned table
A Looker dashboard refresh is hitting the warehouse without partition filters
Storage growth pushed an existing query above the price tier

steps.command: bq query --nouse_legacy_sql 'SELECT query, COUNT(*) AS runs, AVG(total_bytes_billed)/POW(1024,4) AS avg_tib FROM `region-us.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT` WHERE user_email = "looker-bi@example.iam.gserviceaccount.com" AND creation_time > TIMESTAMP_SUB(CURRENT_TIMESTAMP(), INTERVAL 7 DAY) GROUP BY query ORDER BY runs DESC LIMIT 5'
steps.rationale: See exactly which Looker query is repeating
steps.compressed_evidence: Single query repeats 96 times. Query is `SELECT * FROM analytics.events` with no WHERE clause and no partition pruning. analytics.events is partitioned on event_date but the query reads all 47 TiB every run. Looker schedule: every 105 minutes.

hypotheses.description: Looker dashboard query against analytics.events with no partition filter, refreshing every 105 minutes

conclude.root_cause: A Looker dashboard refresh runs `SELECT * FROM analytics.events` every 105 minutes, scanning the full 47 TiB partitioned table and billing ~4.5 PiB per week
evidence_summary: One query/service account accounts for >99% of weekly bytes billed
Query has no WHERE clause; partitioned table is fully scanned each run
Refresh cadence is every 105 minutes (96 runs/week)
All other top queries are <1 TiB
not_verified: Which Looker dashboard owns this query (need Looker admin access)
Whether the dashboard actually needs all columns or could be narrowed
recommendations: Pause the offending Looker schedule immediately while you investigate
Add a `WHERE event_date >= CURRENT_DATE - 7` filter and a column subset
Set a per-user query bytes-billed quota on the looker-bi service account
Consider materialized views for recurring dashboard queries

metadata.description: Orphaned persistent disks accumulating after CI cleanup broke

hypotheses.description: Orphaned disks from terminated VMs are accumulating (CI/teardown broken)
A team migrated to larger SSD volumes for a database workload
Increased snapshot retention is bloating storage

steps.rationale: List disks with no attached VM (the `users` field is empty)
steps.compressed_evidence: 184 unattached disks found. 171 are pd-ssd, sized 100-500 GiB. Naming convention 'ci-runner-<sha>-disk' matches CI runners. Creation dates cluster between 2026-03-15 and today — none older than 23 days. Total unattached capacity: 41.8 TiB.

hypotheses.description: CI runner teardown stopped deleting disks around 2026-03-15
A team migrated to larger SSD volumes for a database workload
Increased snapshot retention is bloating storage

steps.rationale: Check whether snapshot count has grown materially
steps.compressed_evidence: Snapshot count: 412 (vs 398 last month). Negligible change. Snapshots are not the source of the spike.

hypotheses.description: CI runner teardown stopped deleting disks around 2026-03-15, leaving 184 orphaned pd-ssd volumes (41.8 TiB)
Increased snapshot retention is bloating storage

conclude.root_cause: CI runner teardown stopped deleting persistent disks on or around 2026-03-15. 184 pd-ssd volumes (41.8 TiB) are now orphaned, all matching the 'ci-runner-*' naming pattern.
evidence_summary: 184 unattached disks found, all named 'ci-runner-<sha>-disk'
Creation dates cluster from 2026-03-15 onward — nothing older
171 are pd-ssd at $0.17/GiB-month, totalling 41.8 TiB
Snapshot count is unchanged, ruling out alternative causes
not_verified: What changed in the CI pipeline on 2026-03-15
Whether any of these disks contain data worth preserving
recommendations: Audit the CI teardown script for changes around 2026-03-15
Snapshot then delete the orphaned ci-runner-* disks (script-friendly via labels)
Add a scheduled job to alert on >10 unattached disks older than 24h
Tag CI disks with TTL labels and run a janitor that enforces them

metadata.description: GKE autoscaler stuck scaling up due to a pod that never schedules

hypotheses.description: Cluster autoscaler is repeatedly adding nodes for a pod that can never schedule
A new high-replica deployment legitimately needs more nodes
Node pool moved from spot to on-demand instances

steps.rationale: Compare the current node count to the original target
steps.compressed_evidence: currentNodeCount: 142. initialNodeCount: 18. autoscaling enabled, max=200. Cluster is 8x its baseline size and approaching the autoscaler ceiling. Last expansion event: 4 minutes ago.

hypotheses.description: Cluster autoscaler is repeatedly adding nodes for a pod that can never schedule
A new high-replica deployment legitimately needs more nodes
Node pool moved from spot to on-demand instances

steps.rationale: Look at recent scale-up triggers — what pod is requesting capacity?
steps.compressed_evidence: 20 scale-up events in the last hour. ALL 20 cite the same pending pod: 'ml-training-job-7f3a2' in namespace 'data-science'. Each event adds 8 nodes. The pod requests 96 vCPU + 1 nvidia-tesla-v100 GPU. The node pool has no GPU SKUs configured — the pod cannot ever schedule.

hypotheses.description: ml-training-job-7f3a2 requests a GPU the node pool doesn't offer; autoscaler keeps adding (unusable) nodes trying to schedule it

conclude.root_cause: A pending pod 'ml-training-job-7f3a2' in the data-science namespace requests 1x nvidia-tesla-v100 GPU. The prod-east cluster has no GPU node pools, so the pod is unschedulable — but the cluster autoscaler interprets the pending pod as capacity pressure and keeps adding standard nodes (now at 142 of 200 max)
evidence_summary: Cluster grew 8x from baseline (18 → 142 nodes), nearing the 200-node ceiling
Every scale-up event in the last hour cites the same pending pod
That pod requests a GPU resource the cluster's node pools cannot provide
All 142 nodes are running but nearly idle — the trigger pod is still pending
not_verified: Whether the ml-training-job was supposed to run on a different cluster
How the GPU pod ended up scheduled to prod-east in the first place
recommendations: Delete or evict the ml-training-job-7f3a2 pod immediately to halt further scale-up
Lower the cluster autoscaler max node count as a safety net
Add a node selector / taint so GPU workloads cannot land on prod-east
Alert on cluster size > 2x baseline for more than 30 minutes

metadata.description: NAT Gateway data-transfer cost blowup from missing S3 VPC endpoint

hypotheses.description: NAT Gateway data-transfer spike from a workload pushing large S3 traffic through NAT instead of a VPC endpoint
New compute workload with unrelated inter-AZ / internet egress traffic
Cross-region replication or large dataset download driving legitimate NAT growth

steps.rationale: Inventory NAT gateways and their VPC attachments. EC2 - Other groups NAT charges under the EC2 service; we need the gateway list to scope CloudWatch metrics and VPC endpoints next.
steps.compressed_evidence: 3 NAT gateways in vpc-0a1b2c (prod-east). Gateway nat-prod-a owns 78% of VPC traffic by subnet routing. Created 2026-01-15, no tags change recently.

hypotheses.description: NAT Gateway data-transfer spike from a workload pushing large S3 traffic through NAT instead of a VPC endpoint
New compute workload with unrelated inter-AZ / internet egress traffic
Cross-region replication or large dataset download driving legitimate NAT growth

steps.command: aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace AWS/NATGateway --metric-name BytesOutToDestination --dimensions Name=NatGatewayId,Value=nat-prod-a --start-time 2026-02-01T00:00:00Z --end-time 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --period 86400 --statistics Sum
steps.rationale: Get the egress-byte trend for the dominant NAT. A sharp step (vs. gradual growth) points at a single workload being misrouted; gradual growth points at organic traffic.
steps.compressed_evidence: BytesOutToDestination for nat-prod-a: stable ~180 GB/day through 2026-02-14, then jumps to ~2.4 TB/day on 2026-02-15 and stays there. 13x step-change, no ramp. Date matches the spike onset in billing.

hypotheses.description: NAT Gateway data-transfer spike from a workload pushing large S3 traffic through NAT instead of a VPC endpoint
New compute workload with unrelated inter-AZ / internet egress traffic
Cross-region replication or large dataset download driving legitimate NAT growth

conclude.root_cause: A workload in the prod-east VPC (subnet-0f3a2) started reading ~2.4 TB/day from S3 on 2026-02-15, but the VPC has no S3 gateway endpoint. All traffic is routed through NAT Gateway nat-prod-a, billed at ~$0.045/GB processing + $0.09/GB inter-AZ. A free S3 gateway endpoint would remove both charges entirely.
evidence_summary: EC2 - Other (NAT) line item jumped 312% ($3.2K -> $13.2K) starting 2026-02-15
nat-prod-a carries 78% of VPC traffic; its BytesOutToDestination went from 180 GB/day to 2.4 TB/day on the same date (step-change, no ramp)
aws ec2 describe-vpc-endpoints in vpc-0a1b2c returns zero gateway endpoints (no S3, no DynamoDB)
S3 buckets receiving the traffic are in the same region (us-east-1) as the workload — a gateway endpoint would fully eliminate NAT routing for this traffic
not_verified: Which specific EC2 instance or service started the S3 traffic on 2026-02-15
Whether the workload is still needed or was a one-off data migration
recommendations: Create a gateway-type S3 VPC endpoint in vpc-0a1b2c and associate the prod-east route tables (free; no data-processing or inter-AZ charges)
If the S3 buckets span multiple regions, also add a DynamoDB gateway endpoint if any DynamoDB traffic uses NAT
Monitor AWS/NATGateway BytesOutToDestination per gateway; alert on sustained >50% deltas vs. 7-day baseline
Set a Cost Anomaly Detection monitor on EC2 - Other so the next NAT-traffic regression is caught the same week

metadata.description: S3 storage blowup from missing lifecycle policy

hypotheses.description: A bucket with no lifecycle policy is accumulating old data (logs / backups) in STANDARD tier
Legitimate new data-product driving storage growth
Rogue / test workload uploading unbounded data

steps.command: aws s3api list-buckets --output json | jq '.Buckets | sort_by(.CreationDate) | reverse | .[0:20]'
steps.rationale: Start with the 20 most recently created or modified buckets. Storage spikes almost always come from a small number of buckets, and the culprit is usually either new or growing faster than the rest.
steps.compressed_evidence: 41 buckets in the account. Top 5 by recent activity: prod-logs (created 2024), data-lake-raw (2023), ml-features-v2 (2025-11), backup-archive (2022), tmp-analytics (2026-02).

hypotheses.description: A bucket with no lifecycle policy is accumulating old data (logs / backups) in STANDARD tier
Legitimate new data-product driving storage growth
Rogue / test workload uploading unbounded data

steps.command: aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace AWS/S3 --metric-name BucketSizeBytes --dimensions Name=BucketName,Value=prod-logs Name=StorageType,Value=StandardStorage --start-time 2026-01-01T00:00:00Z --end-time 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z --period 86400 --statistics Average
steps.rationale: prod-logs stands out by name. Chart its size over the past 3 months — a linear-growth curve means no lifecycle / retention is pruning; a flat line would refute this hypothesis.
steps.compressed_evidence: prod-logs StandardStorage grew from 48 TB (2026-01-01) to 142 TB (2026-04-01). Linear +1 TB/day. No IntelligentTiering, Glacier, or DeepArchive storage types reported for this bucket.

hypotheses.description: A bucket with no lifecycle policy is accumulating old data (logs / backups) in STANDARD tier
Legitimate new data-product driving storage growth
Rogue / test workload uploading unbounded data

conclude.root_cause: The prod-logs bucket has no lifecycle configuration. It's growing at ~1 TB/day in STANDARD storage — application logs that are almost never read after 30 days. 94 TB of growth over 3 months explains the $7.4K month-over-month delta at $0.023/GB-month. A lifecycle policy transitioning objects to IA after 30 days and Glacier after 90 days would cut the marginal storage cost by ~75%.
evidence_summary: Amazon S3 line item rose 180% ($4.1K -> $11.5K) month-over-month
prod-logs StandardStorage grew linearly from 48 TB to 142 TB over 3 months (+1 TB/day)
aws s3api get-bucket-lifecycle-configuration --bucket prod-logs returned NoSuchLifecycleConfiguration
Access pattern analysis: 98% of objects older than 30 days have zero GETs in the last 60 days
not_verified: Compliance/legal retention requirements for the application logs (may limit how aggressively we can tier)
Whether downstream analytics pipelines rely on objects being in STANDARD tier
recommendations: Add a lifecycle policy on prod-logs: transition to STANDARD_IA at 30 days, GLACIER_IR at 90 days, expire at 365 days (adjust based on compliance)
Enable S3 Storage Lens at the account level to get ongoing visibility into bucket growth and access patterns
Consider S3 Intelligent-Tiering for buckets with uncertain access patterns (automatic, no operational overhead)
Set a Cost Anomaly Detection monitor on Amazon S3 so storage regressions are caught within days, not weeks
